hero.tagline: How a robot perceives the world - and how to pick the right sensor
hero.intro: A robot is only as smart as what it can sense. Cameras give pixels, LiDAR gives ranges, IMUs give acceleration, encoders give joint angles - each sensor has a job and a failure mode. This branch is the field guide: how each sensor actually works, what its noise looks like, when to pick one over another, how to calibrate it, and how to wire it up to ROS. Pick the right sensor and the rest of your stack gets easier; pick the wrong one and no algorithm will save you.

sections.heading: Sensor taxonomy
sections.body: Robot sensors split along two axes. Proprioceptive sensors measure the robot itself (encoders, IMU, joint torques); exteroceptive sensors measure the world (cameras, LiDAR, GPS). Within those, sensors range from passive (cameras, mics - sample ambient signals) to active (LiDAR, sonar, structured-light depth - emit and measure return).
bullets: Proprioceptive: encoders, IMU, current sensors, FT sensors
Exteroceptive: cameras, LiDAR, sonar, GPS, microphones
Active vs passive: active sensors burn power but work in the dark and outdoors better
Sample rate matters - control loops need fast updates, perception can be slower

sections.heading: Cameras - RGB, RGB-D, stereo, event
sections.body: Cameras are the cheapest, densest sensor. Your choice depends on what you need to measure.
bullets: Mono RGB - 2D images. Fine for classification, marker tracking, learned policies
Stereo (ZED, OAK-D) - depth from disparity, works outdoors, no IR contamination
RGB-D structured light (RealSense D4xx, Azure Kinect) - accurate up close indoors
Time-of-flight (Kinect v2, some L5xx) - cleaner depth at range, breaks in sunlight
Event / neuromorphic (Prophesee, iniVation) - microsecond latency, no motion blur, radically different SDK
Thermal (FLIR Lepton, Boson) - sees humans, leaks, fires; not for geometry

codeBlocks.title: OpenCV - read frames from any V4L2 camera
codeBlocks.code:
import cv2
cap = cv2.VideoCapture(0)
cap.set(cv2.CAP_PROP_FRAME_WIDTH, 1280)
cap.set(cv2.CAP_PROP_FRAME_HEIGHT, 720)
cap.set(cv2.CAP_PROP_FPS, 30)
while cap.isOpened():
    ok, frame = cap.read()
    if not ok: break
    cv2.imshow('cam', frame)
    if cv2.waitKey(1) == 27: break

sections.heading: LiDAR - 2D and 3D
sections.body: LiDARs emit laser pulses and measure return time. 2D LiDARs (one rotating beam, sweeping a horizontal plane) are cheap and standard for indoor mobile robots. 3D LiDARs (multiple beams or solid-state arrays) capture full 3D point clouds for outdoor / autonomous-vehicle use.
bullets: 2D mechanical (RPLiDAR, Hokuyo URG, SICK TIM) - $100–2000, ~10 Hz, 1 plane
3D mechanical spinning (Velodyne VLP-16/32, Ouster OS1/2, Robosense) - $5K-50K, 360°
3D solid-state (Livox Mid-360, Innovusion, Hesai) - no moving parts, FOV-limited
Range vs accuracy vs cost - there is no free lunch; pick for your indoor / outdoor / dynamic use case
Eye safety: Class 1 is safe; Class 3R / 3B exists in some industrial units, do not stare at

codeBlocks.title: Velodyne VLP-16 - 16-beam spinning LiDAR
codeBlocks.code:
sudo apt install -y ros-jazzy-velodyne
ros2 launch velodyne velodyne-all-nodes-VLP16-launch.py

codeBlocks.title: Ouster OS1 - modern 32/64-beam, native ROS 2 driver
codeBlocks.code:
cd ~/{{ws}}/src
git clone -b ros2 https://github.com/ouster-lidar/ouster-ros.git
cd ~/{{ws}}
rosdep install --from-paths src --ignore-src -r -y
colcon build --packages-select ouster_ros
source install/setup.bash
ros2 launch ouster_ros driver.launch.py \
    sensor_hostname:=os-122xxxx.local

codeBlocks.title: RPLiDAR A2 - cheap 2D for indoor robots
codeBlocks.code:
sudo apt install -y ros-jazzy-rplidar-ros
ros2 launch rplidar_ros rplidar_a2_launch.py \
    serial_port:=/dev/ttyUSB0

sections.heading: IMU - accelerometer + gyroscope + (sometimes) magnetometer
sections.body: An IMU measures linear acceleration and angular velocity. Cheap MEMS IMUs (BNO055, ICM-20948, MPU-6050) are everywhere; tactical-grade fiber-optic IMUs cost thousands. Raw integration drifts fast - IMUs are almost always fused with another sensor (camera for VIO, GPS, encoders, LiDAR) before they're useful.
bullets: Accelerometer - measures specific force, including gravity. Read tilt at rest, motion in flight
Gyroscope - angular velocity. Drifts in orientation over seconds-minutes
Magnetometer - yaw reference, sensitive to nearby motors and metal
9-DoF IMU = accel + gyro + mag. 6-DoF = no mag
Allan variance characterizes noise + bias instability - read the datasheet's plot

codeBlocks.title: imu_filter_madgwick - fuse 9-DoF into orientation
codeBlocks.code:
imu_filter:
  ros__parameters:
    use_mag: true
    publish_tf: false
    world_frame: enu
    fixed_frame: imu_link
    gain: 0.1

sections.heading: Joint encoders
sections.body: Encoders measure rotational position of a motor or joint. Three families: incremental optical (counts pulses, needs homing, the cheapest), absolute (knows position at power-on, more expensive), and magnetic (Hall + magnet, robust but lower resolution). Resolution is reported as counts per revolution (CPR) - typical robot servos are 4096 to 17-bit (131072) per turn.
bullets: Incremental - count A/B quadrature pulses, plus an index pulse for homing
Absolute - read position directly via SSI, BiSS, EnDat, or analog
Magnetic (AS5048, AS5600) - cheap, contactless, works on motor shafts
Resolver - analog, rugged, common in industrial servos
Quadrature decoding done in hardware (STM32 timer, Pi Pico PIO) saves CPU

codeBlocks.title: Pi Pico - quadrature encoder via PIO (MicroPython)
codeBlocks.code:
from machine import Pin
from rp2 import PIO, StateMachine, asm_pio

@asm_pio()
def quadrature():
    wrap_target()
    in_(pins, 2)            # read 2 pins (A, B)
    push(noblock)
    wrap()

sm = StateMachine(0, quadrature, freq=2_000_000,
                  in_base=Pin(2))   # A on GP2, B on GP3
sm.active(1)

count = 0
last = 0
while True:
    if sm.rx_fifo():
        v = sm.get() & 0x3
        # transition table for quadrature
        delta = ((v - last) & 0x3)
        if delta == 1: count += 1
        elif delta == 3: count -= 1
        last = v

codeBlocks.title: STM32 - TIM2 in encoder mode (HAL)

sections.heading: Force / torque & tactile sensors
sections.body: Once a robot makes contact, you need to measure the contact. Wrist FT sensors (ATI Mini-40, Robotiq FT300) give 6-axis force/torque at the end-effector; great for impedance control and assembly. Tactile sensors (GelSight, DIGIT, Xela) give per-pixel pressure or full vision-based imaging of the contact patch - used in dexterous manipulation research.
bullets: 6-axis FT sensor - strain gauges in a Maltese cross. ~$2K-15K. Bias drifts with temperature
GelSight / DIGIT - camera looks at a deformable gel. Visual, slow (~30 Hz), incredibly rich
Capacitive arrays (Xela, RoboSkin) - coverage over robot 'skin', moderate resolution
Current sensing on the motors - cheapest 'force' proxy if you have the dynamics model

sections.heading: GPS / GNSS / RTK for outdoor robots
sections.body: Standard GPS is ~3 m accurate - fine for cars, useless for centimeter-precise navigation. RTK GPS uses a fixed base station broadcasting corrections to a rover; the rover then resolves carrier-phase ambiguities and gets ~2 cm accuracy. Standard-issue for outdoor agricultural / aerial / autonomous-driving robots.
bullets: u-blox F9P, Septentrio Mosaic, Trimble - common RTK receivers
NTRIP - internet protocol for streaming RTK corrections from public networks
Dual-antenna GPS gives heading without a magnetometer (great for cars, useless under trees)
GPS-denied = inside, under bridges, urban canyons - fall back to LiDAR / VIO

sections.heading: Calibration - intrinsic, extrinsic, hand-eye
sections.body: Every sensor has parameters that need calibration before its data is usable. Intrinsic calibration is camera-internal (focal length, distortion). Extrinsic calibration is sensor-to-sensor or sensor-to-robot (the rigid transform). Hand-eye calibration is the special case of camera-to-robot-mount, solved by moving the robot through known poses while viewing a target.
bullets: Camera intrinsics - checkerboard or charuco target, ROS camera_calibration tool
Camera-to-LiDAR - Autoware calibration toolkit, or hand-pick correspondences
Hand-eye (camera on wrist) - easy_handeye package, solves AX=XB
IMU + camera (VIO) - Kalibr is the research standard

codeBlocks.title: Hand-eye calibration with easy_handeye
codeBlocks.code:
cd ~/{{ws}}/src
git clone https://github.com/marcoesposito1988/easy_handeye2.git
cd ~/{{ws}}
colcon build --packages-select easy_handeye2
ros2 launch easy_handeye2 calibrate.launch.py \
    eye_in_hand:=true tracking_base_frame:=camera_link \
    tracking_marker_frame:=marker robot_base_frame:=base_link \
    robot_effector_frame:=tool0

sections.heading: Time synchronization - chrony, PTP, ROS time
sections.body: When two sensors disagree on what time it is, fusion breaks. A camera image timestamped 50 ms earlier than the IMU sample taken at the same instant produces wrong VIO. The fix is rigorous time sync - software (NTP / chrony) for ~10 ms, hardware-assisted (PTP) for sub-microsecond, hardware trigger lines for genuine simultaneity.
bullets: NTP / chrony - software time sync over network, ~1-50 ms accuracy, the default
PTP (IEEE 1588) - hardware-timestamped, sub-microsecond on a switched network with PTP-aware NICs
Hardware trigger - one wire fires the camera + IMU at the same instant; gold standard for VIO/SLAM
ROS 2 sim time - when use_sim_time:=true every node uses /clock; mismatched values cause weird bugs
TF tolerates timestamp mismatch up to its buffer length (default 10 s) - but throws warnings

codeBlocks.title: chrony - robot synced to a master computer
codeBlocks.code:
# On the master
sudo apt install -y chrony
sudo sed -i 's/^#allow.*/allow 192.168.1.0\/24/' /etc/chrony/chrony.conf
sudo systemctl restart chrony

# On each robot (set the master IP)
sudo apt install -y chrony
echo 'server 192.168.1.10 iburst' | sudo tee -a /etc/chrony/chrony.conf
sudo systemctl restart chrony
chronyc tracking   # offset should be sub-millisecond

codeBlocks.title: PTP with linuxptp
codeBlocks.code:
sudo apt install -y linuxptp
# Server (grandmaster clock)
sudo ptp4l -i eth0 -m -S
# Client
sudo ptp4l -i eth0 -m -s
# Sync the system clock to the PHC
sudo phc2sys -s eth0 -w -m

sections.heading: Bus protocols - CAN, EtherCAT, RS-485, SPI, I²C
sections.body: Inside a robot, sensors and motor drivers don't speak ROS - they speak fieldbus protocols. CAN bus (250 kbps – 1 Mbps) dominates automotive and most industrial AMRs. EtherCAT (100 Mbps) runs synchronized motion control across many drives. RS-485 / Modbus shows up in cheaper sensors and PLCs. SPI / I²C are MCU-level for IMUs, ADCs, OLED displays.
bullets: CAN - 2-wire differential, robust to noise, broadcast, prioritized arbitration
CAN-FD - flexible data-rate; up to 8 Mbps payload; modern robots use this
EtherCAT - distributed clocks, sub-µs sync across many slaves; required for high-end servos
RS-485 - multi-drop; cheap; used by most lidar drivers and industrial sensors
SPI - fast (10+ Mbps), short distance (PCB), 4-wire; perfect for IMUs
I²C - slow (100 kHz – 1 MHz), 2-wire, addressed; used for sensors, EEPROMs, OLEDs

codeBlocks.title: Read a CAN-bus message via python-can
codeBlocks.code:
import can

bus = can.Bus(channel='can0', interface='socketcan')
for msg in bus:
    if msg.arbitration_id == 0x123:
        # Decode 8-byte payload
        rpm = int.from_bytes(msg.data[0:2], 'little', signed=True)
        cur = int.from_bytes(msg.data[2:4], 'little', signed=True) * 0.01
        print(f'rpm={rpm} cur={cur:.2f} A')

sections.heading: mmWave radar - the all-weather sensor
sections.body: mmWave radar (60-77 GHz) sees through rain, dust, and darkness - where camera and lidar struggle. Modern automotive radars (Continental ARS, Bosch LRR4, Texas Instruments AWRxxxx evaluation modules) deliver 4D point clouds (range, angle, elevation, doppler velocity) at 20+ Hz. Used in self-driving cars, outdoor AMRs, drones, and increasingly in factories for human safety zones.
bullets: Frequencies: 24 GHz (legacy, banned in some regions), 77 GHz (automotive), 60 GHz (industrial)
Doppler velocity per point - instantly tells you what's moving
Sparse vs dense - TI AWR1843 gives ~256 points/frame, Arbe Phoenix gives 30K
Pros: weather-immune, long range, velocity built-in
Cons: poor lateral resolution, multipath ghosts, expensive (~$1K-5K for usable units)
Sensor fusion: radar tells you THERE IS something at velocity v, camera tells you WHAT
